behaviors.description: The speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so that the summary can attribute statements to specific attendees.
behaviors.justification: Speaker label preservation depends on the real speech-to-text API's diarization capability and configuration. A mocked API response could have labels, but the real API might not be configured for speaker diarization, or audio quality might prevent label detection. The failure boundary is at the API transport level, not in local parsing logic. Cannot know if labels are truly preserved without calling the real API with real audio.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real speech-to-text API with a real meeting recording containing multiple speakers. Verify that the returned transcript includes speaker diarization labels (e.g., 'Speaker 1:', 'Speaker 2:') for each turn in the conversation.

behaviors.description: The structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee who was assigned a task during the meeting.
behaviors.justification: The core failure mode is whether the LLM correctly identifies and attributes action items. This is a model judgment task, not a parsing or plumbing issue. Different LLMs or model versions will have different success rates. Mocking the LLM (providing fake responses with all action items) would always pass, hiding the real failure where the LLM misses tasks or attributes them to the wrong person. Non-deterministic: running this test 100 times with the real model might sometimes extract all items, sometimes miss some.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a realistic transcript (real or synthetic) where multiple attendees are explicitly assigned specific tasks ('Alice will prepare the report', 'Bob will schedule the follow-up'). Call the real LLM with the full context and summary prompt. Verify that the 'action_items' section of the output includes at least one task for each attendee who received an assignment.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the 'decisions' section of the summary, not buried in the narrative.
behaviors.justification: The failure depends on the LLM's ability to recognize, extract, and properly categorize key decisions. This is a judgment task—different LLMs will have different success rates at identifying what counts as a 'key decision' vs. background discussion. Mocking the LLM response (with decisions already in the right place) hides whether the real model can actually perform this extraction and categorization. Outcomes vary across runs: the model might correctly categorize one decision but miss or misplace another on a different run.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a transcript with several explicit key decisions (e.g., 'We decided to migrate to AWS next quarter', 'The team agreed to switch from weekly to bi-weekly standup'). Call the real LLM. Verify that all key decisions appear in the 'decisions' section of the structured output, and are not left in the narrative or summary text.

behaviors.description: When the audio file is longer than 60 minutes, the service splits it into segments and processes them sequentially, merging the results into a single coherent summary.
behaviors.justification: The critical failure mode is whether the merged summary is coherent. Splitting and sequential processing can be tested at Integration level with mocked APIs, but coherence—maintaining context across segments, avoiding duplicates, consistent speaker references—requires the LLM to understand and synthesize fragmented information. The real failure is incoherent merged output, even if splitting and processing logic are correct. Mocking the LLM would always produce coherent mock summaries, hiding cases where the real model fails to bridge segments. Outcomes depend on model capability: different models produce different coherence quality.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a real or realistic 90-minute meeting recording. Run the service's splitting and segmentation logic, process each segment through the real speech-to-text API, and merge via the real LLM. Verify that the final summary reads as a single coherent document with no major gaps, no duplicate decision/action items across segments, and consistent attribution of statements to speakers.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output — completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording.
behaviors.justification: While the behavior emphasizes 'completion,' a meaningful end-to-end test must verify that the LLM produces a usable summary. System-level tests (with mocked LLM) would catch file I/O and API failures, but would hide LLM failure modes: the model might refuse the task, produce malformed output, or fail to extract required fields. The MOCK-HIDE test applies: mocking the LLM always returns valid JSON, passing System tests but hiding real failures when the model outputs nonsense or incomplete data. The service is explicitly designed to use an LLM, so validating that the real model produces a structured summary is essential. Non-deterministic: different models or runs may produce different quality/validity of summary.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a real meeting recording (audio file). Execute the full pipeline: upload audio, call the real speech-to-text API, call the real LLM to produce the structured summary (with action items, decisions, attendee attributions), and deliver/return the output. Verify that the service completes without errors and returns a valid structured summary with all required fields populated and no manual intervention required.
